transcript:
Watch the faint blue haze in the first few seconds — that's thirty-two separate lines, all trembling and overlapping. Then a single gold line draws itself confidently across them, and your eye reads it as the "real" gesture, the decision. But look at the code: that gold line isn't drawn at all in the usual sense — it's called "the emergent gesture," and it's literally the weighted average of those thirty-two blue ghosts, calculated in a function named "calculate composite gesture" before any animation even starts. The line was never spontaneous. It was computed, then performed as if it were being invented in real time — notice the stroke dash offset trick that makes a pre-existing path look like it's being written stroke by stroke.

There's even a spot in the code called the "nexus point" — a fixed coordinate every ghost gesture is forced to pass through, so that convergence feels inevitable rather than staged.

So what you're watching isn't a choice happening. It's a choice that already happened, wearing the costume of happening now.
